title: Related Projects
text:
Duckboat exists in a rich ecosystem of Python dataframe and SQL tools. HereвҖҷs how it relates to some of them.

Pandas
The most widely-used Python dataframe library. Pandas uses a method-chaining API with eager evaluation. Duckboat differs in using SQL strings instead of Python method calls, and in evaluating lazily via DuckDB. Convert between them with t.do('pandas') or uck.Table(df).


Polars
A modern, fast dataframe library with a fluent expression API and lazy evaluation. Polars provides strong IDE support with autocomplete on column operations. Duckboat trades that discoverability for zero new API вҖ“ you write SQL directly instead of learning Polars expressions.


Ibis
A portable dataframe API that compiles to SQL for various backends (DuckDB, Postgres, BigQuery, etc.). Ibis provides a Python expression API with IDE support and backend portability. Duckboat is simpler and more direct вҖ“ you write SQL snippets rather than building expressions through a Python API.


DuckDB
Duckboat is a thin wrapper around DuckDBвҖҷs relational API. DuckDB provides the query engine; duckboat adds ergonomic SQL snippet chaining, automatic from prepending, and the do() dispatch mechanism. See the DuckDB integration page for details.

Friendlier SQL
Even Friendlier SQL
Friendly SQL reference



PRQL
A pipelined query language that compiles to SQL. PRQL redesigns SQL syntax to be composable and top-to-bottom readable. Duckboat takes a different approach: rather than replacing SQL syntax, it makes standard SQL composable by chaining snippets through Python.


GoogleвҖҷs Pipe Syntax for SQL
A research proposal for adding pipe operators directly to SQL. Duckboat achieves a similar result at the Python level вҖ“ do() chains SQL snippets in a way thatвҖҷs semantically close to what pipe syntax would provide natively.

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
duckboat currently has two main objects:

Table: A single lazy DuckDB relation. Table.do() chains SQL snippets, auto-wrapping each as SELECT * FROM _prev_ <step>.
Database: A named dict of tables. Database.do() and Database.sql() run SQL with explicit table names. Used for joins.

There is also alias, which wraps a single Table into a Database with a given name (used for self-joins).
The Database object exists only to give tables names for SQL queries. This proposal replaces it with two simpler mechanisms in the do() chain, rolled out in two stages.

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
What changes
The do() chain gains dict as a new argument type. A dict in the chain registers named tables for the next SQL step. After the SQL executes, the names are forgottenвҖ”only the result carries forward.
# Multi-table join
uck.do(
    {"orders": orders_df, "customers": customers_df},
    "select * from orders join customers using (id)",
    "where total > 100",
)

# Mid-chain join
t1.do(
    "where total_amount > 0",
    "select *, pickup_zone_id as zid",
    {"zones": zones_df},
    "join zones on zid = zones.id",
    "select zone_name, avg(total_amount) group by 1",
)

# Self-join
t1.do(
    {"t": t1},
    "select * from t a join t b using (hexid)",
)

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
What gets deprecated

Database class (public API): replaced by dict-in-chain.
alias method: replaced by dict-in-chain.
Database.sql(): replaced by dict + SQL string in chain.
Database.hold(): users materialize individual tables instead.

Database and alias continue to work in this release but are no longer documented as the recommended approach. They can be removed in a future release once the dict syntax has had time to settle.

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
Migration







Before
After




Database(a=df1, b=df2).sql("select ...")
uck.do({"a": df1, "b": df2}, "select ...")


t.alias("name").do("select ...")
t.do({"name": t}, "select ...")


Database(a=df1, b=df2).hold("pandas")
Call .do("pandas") on each table individually

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
What stays

Table: unchanged, still the core object.
Table.do() / uck.do(): unchanged interface, one new dispatch case.
Table.hold(): materialize a single table.

The internal query() function that registers named relations with DuckDB staysвҖ”it is the backend for the dict step.

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
Open questions for Stage 1

Dict scope: should the dict register names only for the next step, or until the end of the chain? Proposed: next step only, to avoid hidden state.
Arrow detection: should we detect __arrow_c_stream__ on dict values to accept any Arrow-compatible tabular object, or just check for types we know (DataFrame, Table, filename)?

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
What changes
A t-string (template string, Python 3.14) in the do() chain is processed as follows:

Walk the templateвҖҷs interpolations.
For each interpolation, use .expression as the table name and .value as the data.
Build a dict from these pairs.
Merge into the current context.
Reconstruct the SQL from the templateвҖҷs static strings and expression names.
Execute and return a Table.

This is exactly the dict step from Stage 1, with the dict built implicitly from variable names.
# Multi-table join
uck.do(
    t"select * from {orders} join {customers} using (id)",
    "where total > 100",
)

# Mid-chain join
t1.do(
    "where total_amount > 0",
    t"join {zones} on zid = zones.id",
    "select zone_name, avg(total_amount) group by 1",
)

# Self-join (same variable referenced twice is fine)
t1.do(
    t"select * from {t1} a join {t1} b using (hexid)",
)

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
Why Stage 2 is separate

Python 3.14 is pre-release as of this writing. Requiring it would limit adoption. The dict syntax from Stage 1 works on any Python version.
The dict syntax validates the design. If the вҖңregister, use, forgetвҖқ semantics work well with dicts, adding t-string sugar is low-risk. If something needs to change, it is better to learn that before adding a second syntax for the same thing.
T-strings are sugar, not substance. They produce the same dict internally. No new semantics, just ergonomics.

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
Relationship between dict and t-string
The t-string step is equivalent to a dict step followed by a SQL string step. This t-string:
t1.do(t"join {zones} on zid = zones.id")
is equivalent to:
t1.do({"zones": zones}, "join zones on zid = zones.id")
The t-string just infers the dict and reconstructs the SQL from the template.

title: Proposal: Unifying the duckboat API with do() Chain Dispatch
text:
Open questions for Stage 2

uck.sql() convenience: should it exist as an alias for uck.do(t"...")? Or is uck.do sufficient as the single entry point?
Minimum Python version: should duckboat bump requires-python to 3.14 when t-string support ships, or should t-strings be an optional feature that works if available?

title: Design Philosophy
text: Duckboat is an experiment in taking SQL seriously as a dataframe API. Rather than wrapping SQL behind Python method chains, it makes SQL itself composable.

title: Design Philosophy
text:
SQL-first
Most Python dataframe libraries (Pandas, Polars, Ibis) create a new API layer on top of SQL. You learn their expression syntax, their method names, their way of doing group-bys and joins. This gives you IDE autocomplete, type checking, and discoverability вҖ“ real benefits.
Duckboat takes a different route: SQL is already a great DSL for data. Millions of people already know it. DuckDB has made it friendlier than ever. The missing piece is composability вҖ“ raw SQL strings arenвҖҷt naturally composable. DuckboatвҖҷs Table.sql() fixes this with one trick: auto-prepending from <table>, which turns SQL snippets into composable fragments.

title: Design Philosophy
text:
Snippet composability
A SQL snippet like where x > 5 isnвҖҷt useful on its own вҖ“ it needs a from clause. Duckboat provides that automatically, so snippets chain naturally:
t.do(
    "where status = 'active'",
    'select id, name, score',
    'order by score desc',
    'limit 10',
)
Each string is a SQL fragment. Duckboat prepends the from clause, chains them through DuckDBвҖҷs relation API, and lets the query planner optimize the whole pipeline. This is semantically similar to GoogleвҖҷs Pipe Syntax proposal for SQL, achieved at the Python level.

title: Design Philosophy
text:
Minimal API surface
The entire library is essentially three things:

Table вҖ“ wraps a DuckDB relation
Database вҖ“ a named dict of tables for multi-table queries
do() вҖ“ chains operations and dispatches on argument type

ThatвҖҷs it. There are no expression builders, no column reference objects, no custom aggregation APIs. The вҖңAPIвҖқ is SQL.

title: Design Philosophy
text:
Tradeoffs
This approach has real costs:
No IDE autocomplete on columns. Column names live inside SQL strings. Your editor canвҖҷt tab-complete t.do('select na|') the way it can with df.name in Pandas or col("name") in Polars. Typos are caught at runtime, not in the editor.
Discoverability. The do() dispatch conventions вҖ“ passing int to extract a scalar, "pandas" to convert, "hide" to suppress display вҖ“ are terse and powerful, but you need to know they exist. They canвҖҷt be discovered by browsing method signatures.
Not ideal for production pipelines. For code that needs to be maintained by a team, reviewed in PRs, and validated by CI, the static analysis benefits of a typed API are significant. Duckboat is better suited to interactive exploration.

title: Design Philosophy
text:
Where duckboat fits
Duckboat is at its best for:

Interactive notebooks where youвҖҷre exploring data and iterating quickly
SQL-fluent teams who donвҖҷt want to learn a new API for ad-hoc analysis
Quick scripts where ceremony and boilerplate slow you down

If you need IDE support, type safety, or backend portability, consider Polars or Ibis. If some operation is easier in those libraries, duckboat makes it straightforward to convert between them.

title: Design Philosophy
text:
A small library, deliberately
Duckboat is small вҖ“ both in API surface and codebase. This is intentional. ItвҖҷs an experiment in one direction of the design space: what happens when you lean fully into SQL as your dataframe API, with minimal Python wrapping?
The answer, I think, is a tool thatвҖҷs surprisingly pleasant for interactive work. Not for everything, but for the kind of exploratory data analysis where youвҖҷre thinking in SQL anyway.

title: Foursquare POI Data Example
text:
Foursquare has recently released an interesting Point of Interest (POI) Dataset, which is what we will play with in this example.
Fused.io has made that data readily available, through Source Cooperative which is how we will access it.
import duckboat as uck

title: Foursquare POI Data Example
text:
Load the data
We can download the data with duckboat and save it to a file for quick iteration.

# NOTE: You can comment out the following two lines once
# you have the data saved locally to avoid downloading it again
t = uck.Table('s3://us-west-2.opendata.source.coop/fused/fsq-os-places/2024-12-03/places/10.parquet').hide()
t.save('data/fsq_places_10.parquet')

With the data saved to a file, we can create a duckboat table from a local Parquet file.

t = uck.Table('data/fsq_places_10.parquet')

title: Foursquare POI Data Example
text:
Data processing
As noted elsewhere in the docs, we can store data operations in multiple ways. For example, as Python functions or as a list of SQL snippet strings. Here are some examples.

# Collect an operation as a sequence of SQL snippets,
# and parameterize the target H3 resolution.
def latlng_h3(res):
    return [
        f'select h3_latlng_to_cell(latitude, longitude, {res}) as hexid',
        'select h3_h3_to_string(hexid) as hexid',
    ]

# A data processing procedure stored as a list of SQL snippet strings
count_and_order = [
    'select hexid, count(*) as num group by 1',
    'order by num',
]

title: Foursquare POI Data Example
text:
Parameterized evaluation
With our core data transformation logic defined and parameterized, we can run the same transformation over the data at different H3 resolutions.

t.do(latlng_h3(0), count_and_order)

title: Concepts
text:
import duckboat as uck

# read from a remote CSV file
tbl = uck.Table('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mcnakhaee/palmerpenguins/refs/heads/master/palmerpenguins/data/penguins.csv')
tbl = uck.Table('data/penguins.csv')  # read from a local CSV file

df = tbl.df()  # write to Pandas DataFrame
uck.Table(df)  # read from Pandas DataFrame

df.to_parquet('data/penguins.parquet')  # use Pandas to write to a Parquet file
tbl = uck.Table('data/penguins.parquet')  # read from a local Parquet file

tbl_arrow = tbl.arrow()  # write to an Arrow Table
tbl = uck.Table(tbl_arrow)  # read from an Arrow Table
You can also convert via do():
tbl.do('pandas')  # Pandas DataFrame
tbl.do('arrow')   # PyArrow Table

title: Concepts
text:
Databases and Tables
The library revolves around two objects: Table and Database.

Table wraps a DuckDB DuckDBPyRelation. Its core method is .sql(), which auto-prepends from <table> so you can write SQL snippets without specifying a source table. .do() chains operations and dispatches on argument type (strings, functions, lists, type conversions).
Database is a named dictionary of tables. Its .sql() method requires explicit table names, since thereвҖҷs no single table to auto-prepend. Only the tables in the Database are visible to the query. Useful for joins and multi-table queries.

title: Concepts
text:
Laziness
Since all operations are done through DuckDBPyRelation, expressions are evaluated lazily вҖ“ only when you want to display results or materialize output.
If you would like to materialize a Table or Database concretely, use hold():
x.hold(kind='arrow')   # PyArrow Table (or dict of them for Database)
x.hold(kind='pandas')  # Pandas DataFrame (or dict of them for Database)
Or equivalently via do():
x.do('arrow')
x.do('pandas')
When you materialize a Database, you get a dict of materialized tables. Access individual tables with db['table_name'].

title: Concepts
text:
Examples

Chaining
duckboat uses DuckDB to build up Relation expressions through chaining, which DuckDB will then execute after running the entire expression through a query planner to optimize execution.
import duckboat as uck

# uck.Table('https://d37ci6vzurychx.cloudfront.net/trip-data/yellow_tripdata_2010-01.parquet')
uck.Table('yellow_tripdata_2010-01.parquet').do(
    'select *, pickup_latitude as lat, pickup_longitude as lng',
    'select *, h3_latlng_to_cell(lat, lng, 8) as hexid',
    'select hexid, avg(tip_amount) as tip  group by 1',
    'select h3_h3_to_string(hexid) as hexid, tip',
    'where tip between 10 and 20',
    'order by hexid',
)

в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Alternatives
You can also chain by calling .do() multiple times:
uck.Table('data/yellow_tripdata_2010-01.parquet').do(
    'select *, pickup_latitude as lat, pickup_longitude as lng',
).do(
    'select *, h3_latlng_to_cell(lat, lng, 8) as hexid',
).do(
    'select hexid, avg(tip_amount) as tip  group by 1',
).do(
    'select h3_h3_to_string(hexid) as hexid, tip',
).do(
   'where tip between 10 and 20',
).do(
    'order by hexid',
)



Storing procedures
You can store a sequence of steps as a function:
def foo(rel, res=6):
    return rel.do(
        'select pickup_latitude as lat, pickup_longitude as lng, tip_amount',
        f'select h3_latlng_to_cell(lat, lng, {res}) as hexid, tip_amount as tip',
        'select hexid, avg(tip) as tip group by 1',
        'select h3_h3_to_string(hexid) as hexid, tip',
        'where tip > 0',
    )
Then apply it with:
table.do(foo)
Alternatively, store it as a list of SQL strings:
foo_list = [
    'select pickup_latitude as lat, pickup_longitude as lng, tip_amount',
    'select h3_latlng_to_cell(lat, lng, 6) as hexid, tip_amount as tip',
    'select hexid, avg(tip) as tip group by 1',
    'select h3_h3_to_string(hexid) as hexid, tip',
    'where tip > 0',
]
Then apply it with:
table.do(foo_list)
# or equivalently:
table.do(*foo_list)

title: The Arrow PyCapsule Interface: arrow_c_stream
text:
The Python data ecosystem has many DataFrame librariesвҖ”pandas, Polars, PyArrow, DuckDB, cuDFвҖ”and passing tabular data between them has always been awkward. Each library has its own types, and converting between them meant copies, wrappers, or library-specific glue code.
The Data APIs Consortium tried to solve this with the __dataframe__ interchange protocol around 2022. It never quite worked: the implementation had critical bugs with nullable PyArrow dtypes, the spec was complex, and the consortium repo went dormant by late 2023.
The Arrow PyCapsule Interface replaced it. It is simpler, truly zero-copy, and now universally adopted. It is the standard way for a Python object to say вҖңI am tabular dataвҖ”consume me.вҖқ

title: The Arrow PyCapsule Interface: arrow_c_stream
text:
Producing: exporting data
Any object with __arrow_c_stream__ can be consumed by any Arrow-aware library:
import polars as pl
import pyarrow as pa

# Polars DataFrame -> PyArrow Table (zero-copy via PyCapsule)
df = pl.DataFrame({"a": [1, 2, 3], "b": ["x", "y", "z"]})
table = pa.RecordBatchReader.from_stream(df).read_all()

title: The Arrow PyCapsule Interface: arrow_c_stream
text:
Consuming: importing data
Libraries that accept Arrow data call __arrow_c_stream__ automatically:
# PyArrow Table -> Polars (zero-copy)
arrow_table = pa.table({"a": [1, 2, 3]})
df = pl.from_arrow(arrow_table)

# Any __arrow_c_stream__ object -> DuckDB
import duckdb
result = duckdb.sql("SELECT * FROM df")

title: The Arrow PyCapsule Interface: arrow_c_stream
DuckDB Integration
text: duckboat is a thin wrapper around DuckDBвҖҷs relational API. When you need to drop down to DuckDB directly, duckboat exposes its internals as escape hatches.

title: DuckDB Integration
text:
Escape hatches

uck.con вҖ“ the DuckDB connection
Access the underlying DuckDBPyConnection directly:
import duckboat as uck

uck.con  # the shared in-memory DuckDB connection

# Install and load extensions
uck.con.install_extension('spatial')
uck.con.load_extension('spatial')

# Execute arbitrary SQL
uck.con.execute("CREATE TABLE foo AS SELECT 42 AS x")


uck.query() вҖ“ raw SQL queries
Run a SQL query directly on the connection and get back a DuckDBPyRelation:
rel = uck.query('select 1 + 1 as result')
You can pass keyword arguments to make Python objects available as table names:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'x': [1, 2, 3]})
rel = uck.query('select * from df where x > 1', df=df)


t.rel вҖ“ the underlying relation
Every Table wraps a DuckDBPyRelation. Access it directly for DuckDB-native operations:
t = uck.Table({'x': [1, 2, 3]})

t.rel              # the DuckDBPyRelation
t.rel.columns      # column names
t.rel.dtypes       # column types
t.rel.describe()   # summary statistics
t.rel.explain()    # query execution plan

title: DuckDB Integration
section: WhatвҖҷs different from DuckDBвҖҷs relational API
text:
WhatвҖҷs different from DuckDBвҖҷs relational API
Duckboat adds a few ergonomic improvements over using DuckDBвҖҷs Python API directly:








Feature
DuckDB relational API
Duckboat




Connection management
Must create and pass connections
Shared connection handled automatically


SQL from clause
Must write full SQL
Auto-prepended by Table.sql()


Chaining
.query() with manual naming
.do() chains snippets, functions, and more


Output conversion
.df(), .arrow(), etc.
Also via do('pandas'), do('arrow')


Multi-table queries
Register tables manually
Database(name=data) handles registration


Repr safety
Not addressed
hide()/show() for lazy repr control

title: NYC Trips Example Analysis
text:
WeвҖҷll use the NYC Taxi data set to demonstrate analyzing data interactively with Duckboat. WeвҖҷll compare Taxi trip fares by pickup location between January and February 2010.
We discretize locations into hexagonal bins with H3. One of the benefits of using DuckDB directly, is that we can use anything from the DuckDB ecosystem, like its H3 extension.

Loading data
First, load a table from a local Parquet file and preview its contents.

import duckboat as uck
t1 = uck.Table('data/yellow_tripdata_2010-01.parquet')
t2 = uck.Table('data/yellow_tripdata_2010-02.parquet')
t1.do('limit 7')

t1.columns

['vendor_id',
 'pickup_datetime',
 'dropoff_datetime',
 'passenger_count',
 'trip_distance',
 'pickup_longitude',
 'pickup_latitude',
 'rate_code',
 'store_and_fwd_flag',
 'dropoff_longitude',
 'dropoff_latitude',
 'payment_type',
 'fare_amount',
 'surcharge',
 'mta_tax',
 'tip_amount',
 'tolls_amount',
 'total_amount']




Inspecting and transforming data interactively
You can execute one or more SQL expressions with the Table.do() method.
Expressions are in standard DuckDB SQL, with some small changes:

from <table_name> is automatically inserted into the expression for you.
select ... is optional; if omitted, select * will be inserted.

WeвҖҷll see a few examples of what we can do below.
Convert pickup latitude/longitude locations to resolution 8 H3 cells. Resolution 8 cells are about 0.7 km^2 in size.

x = t1.do("""
select
    *,
    h3_latlng_to_cell(pickup_latitude, pickup_longitude, 8)
        as hexid,
""")
x.do('limit 2')

To help organize things, we can collect a sequence of steps as a list of strings.

avg_list = [
    'select *, h3_latlng_to_cell(pickup_latitude, pickup_longitude, 8) as hexid',
    'select hexid, avg(total_amount) as amount, count(*) as num group by 1',
    'select h3_h3_to_string(hexid) as hexid, amount, num',
]

We can apply the sequence of steps in avg_list to table t1 in multiple ways. If Table.do() encounters a list, it will apply each element of the list in sequence recursively, which means each of the following are equivalent:

t1.do(*avg_list)
t1.do(avg_list)
t1.do([avg_list])
t1.do([[avg_list]])

We can also wrap operations in a Python function, which Table.do() can also handle.

def avg_func(tbl):
    """
    tbl: [pickup_latitude, pickup_longitude, total_amount]
    returns: [hexid, amount, num]
    """
    return tbl.do(avg_list)

The following are equivalent:

avg_func(t1)
t1.do(avg_func)
t1.do([avg_func])

We can build up complex nested expressions by combining functions, query strings, and lists, so the following are equivalent:

t1.do(avg_func, 'where num > 100')
t1.do(avg_list, 'where num > 100')
t1.do([avg_func, 'where num > 100'])
t1.do([avg_list, 'where num > 100'])

Joins
We want to compute average fares for hexes and compare them across January and February. We compute the averages like above, but also want to exlude hexes with only a few trips. So we extend the data pipeline to filter out such hexes, and apply the same operation to the datasets for each month.

t1 = uck.Table('data/yellow_tripdata_2010-01.parquet')
t2 = uck.Table('data/yellow_tripdata_2010-02.parquet')

f = [
    'where (pickup_longitude != 0) and (pickup_latitude != 0)',
    'where total_amount > 0',
    avg_list,
    'where num > 100',
]

t1 = t1.do(f)
t2 = t2.do(f)

t2

    , t1.amount

from
    t1
inner join

""").do("""

    , fare_feb - fare_jan

""")

End-to-end example

import duckboat as uck

core = [
    'select *, h3_latlng_to_cell(pickup_latitude, pickup_longitude, 8) as hexid',
    'select hexid, avg(total_amount) as amount, count(*) as num group by 1',
    'select h3_h3_to_string(hexid) as hexid, amount, num',
]

core_with_filters = [
    'where (pickup_longitude != 0) and (pickup_latitude != 0)',
    'where total_amount > 0',
    core,
    'where num > 100',
]

db = uck.Database(
    t1 = uck.Table('data/yellow_tripdata_2010-01.parquet').do(core_with_filters),
    t2 = uck.Table('data/yellow_tripdata_2010-02.parquet').do(core_with_filters),
)

db.do("""
    select
        hexid,
        t1.amount  as  fare_jan,
        t2.amount  as  fare_feb,
    from        t1
    inner join  t2
    using       (hexid)
""",
    'select *, fare_feb - fare_jan as fare_change',
    'order by fare_change',
)

title: DuckDBвҖҷs Arrow API Change: RecordBatchReader
text:
RecordBatchReader is a PyArrow type (pyarrow.RecordBatchReader), not a DuckDB type. It is a streaming interfaceвҖ”a lazy iterator that yields pyarrow.RecordBatch objects one at a time. Data is not materialized until you explicitly consume it.
Key methods:

read_all(): consumes the entire stream and returns a pyarrow.Table
read_next_batch(): returns the next RecordBatch; raises StopIteration at end
schema: property returning the shared schema of the stream

title: DuckDBвҖҷs Arrow API Change: RecordBatchReader
text:
Motivation
From the PR description:

вҖңUsers mostly want to interact with arrow in a streaming fashion. This is especially important to avoid OOM of naively calling arrow().вҖқ

The core reasons:

Memory efficiency: users would call .arrow() on large datasets, materializing everything into memory and getting OOM errors. RecordBatchReader streams data in batches.
Streaming-first philosophy: DuckDBвҖҷs Arrow integration is designed around zero-copy streaming. Returning a reader aligns with this.
Dedicated alternatives exist: fetch_arrow_table() was already available for users who explicitly wanted full materialization.

title: DuckDBвҖҷs Arrow API Change: RecordBatchReader
text:
DuckDB 1.5.0 API Cleanup (Mar 9, 2026)
The change was a breaking change that was not well documented initially, causing significant user confusion (#71, #85, #95 in duckdb-python).
DuckDB 1.5.0 cleaned up the API:

to_arrow_reader() -> RecordBatchReader (new, preferred)
to_arrow_table() -> Table (new, preferred)
arrow() -> RecordBatchReader (kept but discouraged)
Deprecated: fetch_record_batch(), fetch_arrow_table(), fetch_arrow_reader()

title: DuckDBвҖҷs Arrow API Change: RecordBatchReader
text:
One-shot consumption
A RecordBatchReader is single-use. Once you have read from it, the stream is exhausted. You cannot rewind or re-read. If you pass a RecordBatchReader to DuckDB as input (e.g., SELECT * FROM reader), DuckDB will consume it. Any subsequent query against it finds an empty stream.

title: DuckDBвҖҷs Arrow API Change: RecordBatchReader
text:
Deadlocks
When DuckDB returns a RecordBatchReader, it holds a streaming query on that connection. The reader is backed by a StreamQueryResult which starts an execution pipeline that blocks until batches are consumed.
If you then try to execute a new query on the same connection that scans that RecordBatchReader, two things happen:

The new query needs the ClientContextLock to execute.
The RecordBatchReaderвҖҷs GetNextChunk also needs the ClientContextLock to produce data.

This creates a deadlock: the new query holds the lock waiting for data from the reader, while the reader needs the lock to produce data. This was documented in duckdb-python #85, where stack traces showed two threads deadlocked on ResultArrowArrayStreamWrapper::MyStreamGetNext and ArrowArrayStreamWrapper::GetNextChunk.

title: DuckDBвҖҷs Arrow API Change: RecordBatchReader
text:
Workarounds

Materialize first: call .read_all() to consume the reader into a Table before passing it back to DuckDB.
Use to_arrow_table() (1.5.0+) to get a Table directly, bypassing the RecordBatchReader entirely.
Use separate connections if streaming is needed.

title: Duckboat
text:
Ugly to some, but gets the job done.
GitHub | Docs | PyPI
Duckboat is a SQL-based Python dataframe library for ergonomic interactive data analysis and exploration.
pip install duckboat
Duckboat allows you to chain SQL snippets (meaning you can usually omit select * and from ...) to incrementally and lazily build up complex queries.
Duckboat is a light wrapper around the DuckDB relational API, so expressions are evaluated lazily and optimized by DuckDB prior to execution. The resulting queries are fast, avoiding the need to materialize intermediate tables or perform data transfers. You can leverage all the SQL syntax improvements provided by DuckDB: 1 2 3


import duckboat as uck

uck.do(
    'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/allisonhorst/palmerpenguins/main/inst/extdata/penguins.csv',
    "where sex = 'female' ",
    'where year > 2008',
    'select *, cast(body_mass_g as double) as grams',
    'select species, island, avg(grams) as avg_grams group by 1,2',
    'select * replace (round(avg_grams, 1) as avg_grams)',
    'order by avg_grams',
)
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  species  в”Ӯ  island   в”Ӯ avg_grams в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  varchar  в”Ӯ  varchar  в”Ӯ  double   в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Adelie    в”Ӯ Torgersen в”Ӯ    3193.8 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Adelie    в”Ӯ Dream     в”Ӯ    3357.5 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Adelie    в”Ӯ Biscoe    в”Ӯ    3446.9 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Chinstrap в”Ӯ Dream     в”Ӯ    3522.9 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Gentoo    в”Ӯ Biscoe    в”Ӯ    4786.3 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ


We can translate to and from other data formats like Pandas DataFrames, Polars, or Arrow Tables.
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({'a': [0]})
t = uck.Table(df)
t
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   a   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ int64 в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ     0 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Translate back to a pandas dataframe with any of the following:
t.df()
t.hold('pandas')
t.do('pandas')



You can chain calls to Table.do():
f = 'select a + 1 as a'
t.do(f).do(f).do(f)
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   a   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ int64 в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ     3 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Alternatively, Table.do() accepts a sequence of arguments:
t.do(f, f, f)
It also accepts lists of expressions, and will apply them recursively:
fs = [f, f, f]
t.do(fs)
Note, you could also still call this as:
t.do(*fs)
Use lists to group expressions, which Duckboat will apply recursively:
t.do(f, [f], [f, [[f, f], f]])
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   a   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ int64 в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ     6 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Duckboat will also apply functions:
def foo(x):
    return x.do('select a + 2 as a')

# the following are equivalent
foo(t)
t.do(foo)
Of course, you can mix functions, SQL strings, and lists:
uck.do(df, foo, [f, foo])
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   a   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ int64 в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ     5 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ



Use Database to work with multiple named tables and write full SQL (including joins):
import pandas as pd

orders = pd.DataFrame({'id': [1, 2, 3], 'customer_id': [10, 20, 10], 'amount': [5.0, 12.0, 8.0]})
customers = pd.DataFrame({'id': [10, 20], 'name': ['Alice', 'Bob']})

db = uck.Database(orders=orders, customers=customers)
Unlike Table.sql(), Database.sql() requires explicit table names:
db.sql('''
    select c.name, sum(o.amount) as total
    from orders o
    join customers c on o.customer_id = c.id
    group by 1
''')
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  name   в”Ӯ total  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ varchar в”Ӯ double в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Alice   в”Ӯ   13.0 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Bob     в”Ӯ   12.0 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Database.do() works the same way, chaining full SQL expressions:
db.do(
    'select c.name, o.amount from orders o join customers c on o.customer_id = c.id',
    'order by amount desc',
)
You can also create a Database inline via do() by passing a dict:
uck.do(
    {'orders': orders, 'customers': customers},
    'select c.name, sum(o.amount) as total from orders o join customers c on o.customer_id = c.id group by 1',
)
Or alias a single table into a database for joins with itself:
t.do('alias my_table')



do() dispatches on the type of its arguments, providing some handy shortcuts.
Extract scalars, lists, and dicts:
t.do('select count(*)', int)       # returns a Python int
t.do('select distinct a', list)    # returns a Python list
t.do('limit 1', dict)              # returns a Python dict
Convert to other formats:
t.do('pandas')   # returns a Pandas DataFrame
t.do('arrow')    # returns a PyArrow Table
Apply functions:
def add_col(t):
    return t.do("select *, a + 10 as b")

t.do(add_col, 'select b', int)  # 10
Run .sql files:
t.do('queries/transform.sql')  # loads and runs the file contents as SQL
Control display with hide and show:
big = uck.Table('huge_dataset.parquet').do('hide')  # suppresses repr output
big.do('show')  # re-enables repr





Table wraps a DuckDB DuckDBPyRelation. Create one from a file path, URL, Pandas DataFrame, Arrow Table, or dict:
t = uck.Table('data.parquet')
t = uck.Table(pd.DataFrame({'x': [1, 2, 3]}))
The core method is .sql(), which auto-prepends from <table> so you can write SQL snippets:
t.sql('select x + 1 as x')  # becomes: from <table> select x + 1 as x
.do() chains operations and dispatches on argument type (strings, functions, lists, type conversions). Access the underlying DuckDB relation with t.rel.



Database is a named dictionary of tables:
db = uck.Database(orders=orders_df, customers=customers_df)
Unlike Table, Database.sql() requires explicit table names since thereвҖҷs no single table to auto-prepend. Only the tables in the Database are visible to the query. Access individual tables with db['table_name'].



Calling repr() on a Table or Database triggers query evaluation. In Jupyter, this happens when an object is the last expression in a cell. In IDEs like Positron, the variable explorer proactively inspects objects, which can trigger expensive computations.
Use hide() to suppress evaluation:
big = uck.Table('huge_dataset.parquet').do('hide')
# Positron's variable explorer will see: <Table(..., _hide=True)>
# instead of evaluating the full query
Call show() (or .do('show')) when youвҖҷre ready to see results.





Duckboat bets that SQL is already the right language for tabular data manipulation вҖ“ you just need a way to compose SQL snippets into pipelines. This results in a mixture of Python and SQL that is semantically similar to GoogleвҖҷs Pipe Syntax for SQL.
Strengths:

Zero new API to learn. If you know SQL, you know duckboat. There are no new method chains, expression builders, or DSLs to memorize.
Minimal surface area. The library is essentially Table, Database, and .do(). The codebase is small and stays out of your way.
Snippet composability. SQL fragments chain naturally through do(), letting you build complex queries incrementally and interactively.

Tradeoffs:

No IDE autocomplete on column names. Column references live inside SQL strings, so you donвҖҷt get tab-completion or type checking. Typos surface at runtime, not in your editor.
Discoverability. The do() dispatch conventions (int, list, "pandas", "hide", etc.) are terse but must be learned вҖ“ they canвҖҷt be discovered through autocomplete.

Where duckboat fits best:
Duckboat is ideal for interactive exploration and notebook workflows, especially for teams already fluent in SQL. If you need strong static analysis, IDE support, or production-grade type safety, a fluent API like Polars or Ibis may be a better fit. If some operation is easier in another library, duckboat makes it straightforward to translate between them via Pandas, Arrow, or Polars.



IвҖҷd love to hear any feedback on the approach here, so feel free to reach out through Issues or Discussions.

title: Duckboat
text:
import duckboat as uck

uck.do(
    'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/allisonhorst/palmerpenguins/main/inst/extdata/penguins.csv',
    "where sex = 'female' ",
    'where year > 2008',
    'select *, cast(body_mass_g as double) as grams',
    'select species, island, avg(grams) as avg_grams group by 1,2',
    'select * replace (round(avg_grams, 1) as avg_grams)',
    'order by avg_grams',
)
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  species  в”Ӯ  island   в”Ӯ avg_grams в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  varchar  в”Ӯ  varchar  в”Ӯ  double   в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Adelie    в”Ӯ Torgersen в”Ӯ    3193.8 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Adelie    в”Ӯ Dream     в”Ӯ    3357.5 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Adelie    в”Ӯ Biscoe    в”Ӯ    3446.9 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Chinstrap в”Ӯ Dream     в”Ӯ    3522.9 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Gentoo    в”Ӯ Biscoe    в”Ӯ    4786.3 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ


We can translate to and from other data formats like Pandas DataFrames, Polars, or Arrow Tables.
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({'a': [0]})
t = uck.Table(df)
t
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   a   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ int64 в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ     0 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Translate back to a pandas dataframe with any of the following:
t.df()
t.hold('pandas')
t.do('pandas')



You can chain calls to Table.do():
f = 'select a + 1 as a'
t.do(f).do(f).do(f)
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   a   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ int64 в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ     3 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Alternatively, Table.do() accepts a sequence of arguments:
t.do(f, f, f)
It also accepts lists of expressions, and will apply them recursively:
fs = [f, f, f]
t.do(fs)
Note, you could also still call this as:
t.do(*fs)
Use lists to group expressions, which Duckboat will apply recursively:
t.do(f, [f], [f, [[f, f], f]])
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   a   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ int64 в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ     6 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Duckboat will also apply functions:
def foo(x):
    return x.do('select a + 2 as a')

# the following are equivalent
foo(t)
t.do(foo)
Of course, you can mix functions, SQL strings, and lists:
uck.do(df, foo, [f, foo])
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   a   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ int64 в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ     5 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ



Use Database to work with multiple named tables and write full SQL (including joins):
import pandas as pd

orders = pd.DataFrame({'id': [1, 2, 3], 'customer_id': [10, 20, 10], 'amount': [5.0, 12.0, 8.0]})
customers = pd.DataFrame({'id': [10, 20], 'name': ['Alice', 'Bob']})

db = uck.Database(orders=orders, customers=customers)
Unlike Table.sql(), Database.sql() requires explicit table names:
db.sql('''
    select c.name, sum(o.amount) as total
    from orders o
    join customers c on o.customer_id = c.id
    group by 1
''')
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  name   в”Ӯ total  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ varchar в”Ӯ double в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Alice   в”Ӯ   13.0 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Bob     в”Ӯ   12.0 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Database.do() works the same way, chaining full SQL expressions:
db.do(
    'select c.name, o.amount from orders o join customers c on o.customer_id = c.id',
    'order by amount desc',
)
You can also create a Database inline via do() by passing a dict:
uck.do(
    {'orders': orders, 'customers': customers},
    'select c.name, sum(o.amount) as total from orders o join customers c on o.customer_id = c.id group by 1',
)
Or alias a single table into a database for joins with itself:
t.do('alias my_table')



do() dispatches on the type of its arguments, providing some handy shortcuts.
Extract scalars, lists, and dicts:
t.do('select count(*)', int)       # returns a Python int
t.do('select distinct a', list)    # returns a Python list
t.do('limit 1', dict)              # returns a Python dict
Convert to other formats:
t.do('pandas')   # returns a Pandas DataFrame
t.do('arrow')    # returns a PyArrow Table
Apply functions:
def add_col(t):
    return t.do("select *, a + 10 as b")

t.do(add_col, 'select b', int)  # 10
Run .sql files:
t.do('queries/transform.sql')  # loads and runs the file contents as SQL
Control display with hide and show:
big = uck.Table('huge_dataset.parquet').do('hide')  # suppresses repr output
big.do('show')  # re-enables repr





Table wraps a DuckDB DuckDBPyRelation. Create one from a file path, URL, Pandas DataFrame, Arrow Table, or dict:
t = uck.Table('data.parquet')
t = uck.Table(pd.DataFrame({'x': [1, 2, 3]}))
The core method is .sql(), which auto-prepends from <table> so you can write SQL snippets:
t.sql('select x + 1 as x')  # becomes: from <table> select x + 1 as x
.do() chains operations and dispatches on argument type (strings, functions, lists, type conversions). Access the underlying DuckDB relation with t.rel.



Database is a named dictionary of tables:
db = uck.Database(orders=orders_df, customers=customers_df)
Unlike Table, Database.sql() requires explicit table names since thereвҖҷs no single table to auto-prepend. Only the tables in the Database are visible to the query. Access individual tables with db['table_name'].



Calling repr() on a Table or Database triggers query evaluation. In Jupyter, this happens when an object is the last expression in a cell. In IDEs like Positron, the variable explorer proactively inspects objects, which can trigger expensive computations.
Use hide() to suppress evaluation:
big = uck.Table('huge_dataset.parquet').do('hide')
# Positron's variable explorer will see: <Table(..., _hide=True)>
# instead of evaluating the full query
Call show() (or .do('show')) when youвҖҷre ready to see results.

title: Duckboat
text:
Duckboat bets that SQL is already the right language for tabular data manipulation вҖ“ you just need a way to compose SQL snippets into pipelines. This results in a mixture of Python and SQL that is semantically similar to GoogleвҖҷs Pipe Syntax for SQL.
Strengths:

Zero new API to learn. If you know SQL, you know duckboat. There are no new method chains, expression builders, or DSLs to memorize.
Minimal surface area. The library is essentially Table, Database, and .do(). The codebase is small and stays out of your way.
Snippet composability. SQL fragments chain naturally through do(), letting you build complex queries incrementally and interactively.

Tradeoffs:

No IDE autocomplete on column names. Column references live inside SQL strings, so you donвҖҷt get tab-completion or type checking. Typos surface at runtime, not in your editor.
Discoverability. The do() dispatch conventions (int, list, "pandas", "hide", etc.) are terse but must be learned вҖ“ they canвҖҷt be discovered through autocomplete.

Where duckboat fits best:
Duckboat is ideal for interactive exploration and notebook workflows, especially for teams already fluent in SQL. If you need strong static analysis, IDE support, or production-grade type safety, a fluent API like Polars or Ibis may be a better fit. If some operation is easier in another library, duckboat makes it straightforward to translate between them via Pandas, Arrow, or Polars.

title: Lazy Evaluation Tutorial
text:
Introduction
Since duckboat is based on the DuckDB relational API, all expressions are вҖңlazyвҖқ in that they defer evaluation until a result or a data preview is requested. This allows us to build up complex data processing pipelines iteratively, but without needing to compute extranous intermediate results. Instead, under the hood, DuckDB will gather the sequence of steps and pass it to a query optimizer, which will apply optimizations like predicate and projection pushdown. The full operation will be executed by DuckDB making full use of all the cores available on your machine, streaming the operations if possible, and even spilling to disk if the operation is too large to fit in memory.

import duckboat as uck


t0 = uck.Table('data/yellow_tripdata_2010-01.parquet')
t0.columns

['vendor_id',
 'pickup_datetime',
 'dropoff_datetime',
 'passenger_count',
 'trip_distance',
 'pickup_longitude',
 'pickup_latitude',
 'rate_code',
 'store_and_fwd_flag',
 'dropoff_longitude',
 'dropoff_latitude',
 'payment_type',
 'fare_amount',
 'surcharge',
 'mta_tax',
 'tip_amount',
 'tolls_amount',
 'total_amount']



count_rows = "select format('{:.2e}', 1.0*count(*)) as num_rows"
t0.do(count_rows)

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ num_rows в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ varchar  в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ 1.49e+07 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ


The following code is executed instantaneously, since no query operations are performed.

t1 = t0.do(
    'where (pickup_longitude != 0) and (pickup_latitude != 0)',
    'where total_amount > 0',

    'select *, h3_latlng_to_cell(pickup_latitude, pickup_longitude, 12) as hexid',
    'select * replace ( h3_h3_to_string(hexid) as hexid )',
    'select cast(pickup_datetime as timestamp) as ts, hexid, total_amount as amt',
)



Lazy operations
Listing the table as the last expression in a Jupyter notebook makes Jupyter try to represent the table, which triggers DuckDB do either compute the full table, or, in the case that the table has many rows, compute just enough rows to show a preview. In many instances, the preview is faster to compute.
The following is still fast, but just a bit slower than the previous cell, since this is where the query associated with the operations above is actually performed.

t1

Avoiding Expensive Intermediates
However, running count_rows on t2 forces the full join operation to be performed (previously, we only computed a partial join to dispaly the preview). The following takes about 50 seconds on my laptop.
Note that the row count for this intermediate table is about 10 billion rows. We deal with the table directly here for demonstration purposes, but we as we continue the pipeline below, we will avoid ever forming this intermediate table.

# renders slowly because you have to do the full join
t2.do(count_rows)

Again, it is intantaneous to form the expression representing t3, as long as we donвҖҷt need to compute the expression just yet.
Note that the timestamp filtering below could have also been given above as part of the join. WeвҖҷre free to do it either way and the performance will be identical because DuckDB will push the filters down in its query planning/optimization step.

t3 = t2.do(
    'where ts1 < ts2',
    'where ts2 < ts1 + interval 1 minute',
    'select hexid, max(abs(amt1-amt2)) as diff group by 1',
    'where diff > 0'
    'order by diff',
    'hide',
)

Note that this is faster than the t2.do(count_rows), even though it does more work! This cell runs in about 44 seconds on my laptop.


Materialize smaller results
This final result has about 29 thousand rows, something much more reasonable to materialize directly as a Pandas dataframe, for instance.

title: Lazy Evaluation
text: Since duckboat builds on top of DuckDBвҖҷs relational API, all expressions are lazily evaluated by default. This means that complex query expressions can be built up interactively and step by step, and when you are ready to evaluate the final expression (or any intermediate expressions), the query steps will be assembled and optimized by the query planner to allow for the most efficient execution possible:

title: Lazy Evaluation
text:
Example
import duckboat as uck

# Nothing is computed yet -- this just records the chain of operations
t = uck.Table('big_dataset.parquet').do(
    "where category = 'A'",
    'select id, value, value * 2 as double_value',
    'order by value desc',
    'limit 100',
)

# Still lazy -- t is a Table wrapping a DuckDBPyRelation
type(t)  # <class 'duckboat.table.Table'>

# Evaluation is triggered when you display the result...
t  # in a notebook cell, this calls repr() and runs the query

# ...or when you materialize:
df = t.do('pandas')  # now the query actually executes
DuckDBвҖҷs query planner sees the entire chain and can optimize across steps. For example, the limit 100 can be pushed down so DuckDB never materializes more than necessary.

title: Lazy Evaluation
text:
Materializing results
Use hold() to explicitly materialize a lazy table:
arrow_table = t.hold(kind='arrow')   # PyArrow Table
pandas_df = t.hold(kind='pandas')    # Pandas DataFrame
Or equivalently via do():
t.do('arrow')
t.do('pandas')
For a Database, hold() materializes all tables and returns a dict.

title: Lazy Evaluation
text:
Gotchas

Notebook cells
In Jupyter or IPython, having a Table as the last expression in a cell calls repr(), which triggers evaluation. This is usually fine вҖ“ itвҖҷs how you see your results. But be aware that each cell display re-runs the query.


IDE variable explorers
IDEs like Positron proactively inspect objects in the namespace to show them in the variable explorer. This calls repr() on your tables, which triggers query evaluation вҖ“ potentially expensive for large datasets or complex queries.
Solution: use hide() to suppress evaluation:
big = uck.Table('huge_dataset.parquet').do(
    'select ...',
    'where ...',
    'hide',  # suppress repr
)

# Positron sees: <Table(..., _hide=True)>
# The query is NOT evaluated

# When you're ready to see results:
big.do('show')
You can also call hide() and show() as methods:
big = uck.Table('huge_dataset.parquet').hide()
big.show()  # re-enables repr
